what_it_does: This file is a post-reboot operational runbook that lists the minimal commands and service loadouts needed to bring the Company runtime back after a full WSL or computer restart, while distinguishing what auto-starts, what persists on disk, and what must be manually recreated.
observations: A reboot loses no files — git tree, .data/store, vaults, and ~/.claude transcripts survive; only live processes, VRAM-resident models, and live agent sessions are lost.
Almost nothing Company-side auto-starts on boot by design; only ollama, tailscaled, and memory-guard are enabled to start automatically.

The minimal sequence is: company up, optional company up session-supervisor, company up @xsession-brain, optional company up jobs, start phone surfaces manually, cd /home/tim/company && claude, then let the lead respawn fleet members.
A fresh claude session reloads the company MCP server, which is required for a recent routing fix for concurrent cloud models to take effect.
Cloud models currently return empty content for structured-output extraction, so reliable concurrent extraction still needs local GPU models.
Two recall layers are explicitly distinguished: a 35,904-vector chroma transcript vault-mine and a separate 52,875-vector FsStore extractions meaning-recall index.
The document flags a stale ops/WINDOWS-BOOT.md, a resolved phone-surface mapping, the absence of a one-command fleet relaunch, and several minor cleanups.
Services are driven by a company CLI shim that reads ops/services.json and controls systemd --user units.
standouts: The one-line truth that reboot loses zero files.
Step 4 alone (claude) gives a working lead with full mcp__company tools off disk.
Cloud concurrent model routing is fixed, but structured-output extraction on cloud models is still broken.
The intentional minimal-auto-start design and lead-only fleet respawn law.
Explicit two-layer clarification separating the chroma transcript vault from the FsStore meaning-recall index.
conventions: Markdown runbook format with sections, code blocks, and bullet lists.
Live system state is declared authoritative over stale documentation.
Inline dated flags and owner attributions (e.g., Tim 2026-06-06).
Self-contained operational doc written for a specific audience (the lead and Tim).
Uses project-specific CLI verbs, service groups, and @loadout combos.
concerns: Dense parenthetical asides and nested clarifications may make emergency scanning difficult.
Many notes are tied to specific recent dates and transient fixes, so the doc may age quickly.
Operational commands are mixed with deep architectural commentary and incident post-mortem notes.
Some services lack full unit coverage (rerank-jina is started by a manual script).
Assumes Tim and the lead are available; no documented fallback if neither is present.
notes: This is the post-reboot checklist: start canvas and bridge, optionally load the GPU brain and jobs, launch claude, and let the lead respawn the fleet.
questions: What is the exact structure of ops/services.json and how does the company CLI map targets to systemd units?
Where are the unit file definitions for each service stored and what do they contain?
What is the schema and content of .data/store, .data/channels, and ~/.claude transcript files?
How does mcp_face/server.py register and load the mcp__company tools?
What is the intended design decision for default-model cognition: keep requiring the local 4b or default to cloud?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to be a self-contained post-reboot runbook; the content matches that purpose but also embeds extensive architectural commentary and recent incident notes, making it part runbook and part living decision log.
apparent_themes: Minimal automatic boot by explicit design.
Live empirical state wins over documentation.
Separation of durable disk state from ephemeral runtime state.
Clear layering of memory and recall systems.
intention_signals: It is trying to eliminate post-reboot guesswork by giving Tim a short, verified command sequence and by recording recent fixes and gaps so the lead does not have to rediscover them.
novelty: It is unusually explicit about what does not auto-start, documents a lead-only spawn law for the agent fleet, and includes live corrections of its own stale documentation plus a two-layer recall clarification.
contribution: It contributes a durable, self-describing boot sequence and a record of which state survives reboot versus what must be manually restored, along with flags for follow-up cleanup and design decisions.
summary_for_embedding: BOOT-RUNBOOK.md is a post-reboot operational guide listing the minimal commands to restore the Company canvas, bridge, optional GPU brain, jobs, phone surfaces, and lead session after a WSL or computer restart, while noting what auto-starts and what persists on disk.
intention_for_embedding: It was written to give Tim and any relaunched session a verified, self-contained sequence for bringing the Company runtime back after reboot without relying on stale documentation.
